q: Deep Blue’s chess success largely relied on:
options: Performing huge numbers of simple calculations/search very quickly
explain: It searched possible moves at massive scale — not a general theory of everyday intelligence.
hint: It searched possible moves at massive scale — not a general theory of everyday intelligence.

q: If-this-then-that (IFTT) style programming works best when:
options: The environment is open-ended and unpredictable
The problem space is fixed/constrained so responses can be pre-specified
explain: Chess boards and assembly lines are constrained; everyday cognition is not fully pre-specifiable.
hint: Chess boards and assembly lines are constrained; everyday cognition is not fully pre-specifiable.

q: Richard Feynman’s line “What I cannot create I do not understand” is used to argue that:
options: Building working models/machines is a strong test of understanding
explain: If we truly understand a system, we should be able to recreate or model its behavior — AI progress gauges cognitive understanding.
hint: If we truly understand a system, we should be able to recreate or model its behavior — AI progress g

q: The chapter’s three broad approaches to studying cognition are:
options: Neuroscience, cognitive psychology, and computational modeling
explain: LO 1.4: neuroscience, cognitive psychology, and computational modeling form the three-pronged approach.
hint: LO 1.4: neuroscience, cognitive psychology, and computational modeling form the three-pronged approa

q: Neuroscience approaches contribute by:
options: Linking cognitive functions to neural structures, activity, and mechanisms
explain: Brain measures (and related methods) constrain and complement behavioral theories of cognition.
hint: Brain measures (and related methods) constrain and complement behavioral theories of cognition.

q: Cognition is best defined as:
options: The acquisition and processing of sensory information about the world in order to make behavioral decisions
explain: The text defines cognition as intelligent mental activities: acquiring and processing sensory information to make behavioral decisions — not lower-level vegetative functions.
hint: The text defines cognition as intelligent mental activities: acquiring and processing sensory inform

q: Basic research in cognition aims to:
options: Only build products with no curiosity
Understand the world/phenomena without requiring a specific end-use application
Avoid all applications forever by law
explain: Basic research seeks understanding (who we are, how cognition works); applications may follow but are not the defining goal.
hint: Basic research seeks understanding (who we are, how cognition works); applications may follow but ar

q: Applied research in cognition is concerned with:
options: Only pure philosophy with no goals
Developing an application or solution to a problem
explain: Applied work targets uses: clinical disorders, education, human factors, AI systems, etc.
hint: Applied work targets uses: clinical disorders, education, human factors, AI systems, etc.

q: A key reason cognitive scientists argue that studying the brain alone is not enough to explain the mind is that:
options: The brain's staggering complexity means current understanding of it remains poor
Neurons are too small to be measured with any current technology
The brain plays no causal role in producing behavior
Only subjective introspection can reveal how neurons work
explain: Because the brain is so complex (tens of billions of neurons and hundreds of trillions of connections), scientific understanding of it is still poor, so neuroscience alone cannot fully explain cognition. Individual neurons can in fact be measured, so the first option is false. The claim that the brain plays no causal role contradicts the whole enterprise. Introspection is not required to study neurons and is a separate, limited method.
hint: Consider what the sheer scale of the brain implies about how well we understand it.

q: Which set of examples all fall under phenomenon-driven research?
options: Donders' reaction-time tasks, the imagery debate, and Donders' subtraction logic
Signal detection theory, human factors, and radar-operator research
Visual illusions, the Flashed Faces Distortion Effect, and the Stroop effect
Independent variables, dependent variables, and the speed-accuracy tradeoff
explain: Visual illusions, the Flashed Faces Distortion Effect, and the Stroop effect are all cited as phenomena discovered first and then investigated. Donders' tasks and the imagery debate are hypothesis-driven examples. Signal detection and human factors belong to applied cognitive psychology. IVs, DVs, and the speed-accuracy tradeoff are experimental-methods concepts, not phenomena.
hint: Look for effects that were noticed before they were explained.

q: In computing, what is a "function"?
options: A physical circuit made of transistors
A mapping from input to output
A person trained to follow arithmetic rules
A visual diagram of processing stages
explain: A function is any consistent mapping from input to output, such as addition mapping numbers to their sum. A physical circuit is hardware, not the abstract mapping. A person following rules describes the historical human "computer," not the function itself. A visual diagram of stages is a flowchart, a separate concept.
hint: Think about what links what goes in to what comes out.

q: The question of how mental events such as thoughts, beliefs, and sensations relate to or are caused by physical processes in the body is known as the:
explain: The mind-body problem is exactly the puzzle of how mental events relate to or are caused by physical mechanisms in the brain. The knowledge argument is Jackson's specific thought experiment about color, not the general problem. The cognitive revolution was the historical shift toward viewing cognition as computation. The speed-accuracy tradeoff is an experimental measurement concern, unrelated to the mind-body question.
hint: Think about the broad philosophical question that all the mind-brain positions are trying to answer.

q: A view holding that the mind and the body are fundamentally different kinds of substance — one physical and one non-physical — is called:
explain: Dualism holds that mind and body are two fundamentally different kinds of substance, the body physical and the mind non-physical. Physicalism (materialism) says only physical reality exists. Neutral monism posits a single substance that is neither physical nor mental. Idealism says only mental reality exists. Each of the wrong options is a monist view, which by definition denies the two-substance claim.
hint: The prefix means 'two' — count the kinds of substance involved.

q: A researcher knows every physiological, chemical, and physical fact about how the human visual system processes the color yellow, but she is color-blind and has never personally seen yellow. When she finally experiences yellow, a philosopher argues she has just learned something genuinely new. This argument is used to support:
explain: This is Jackson's (1986) knowledge argument, or the color-blind scientist case. If she learns something new upon experiencing the color, then complete physical knowledge left something out — the personal mental experience — which supports dualism. A physicalist would say she already understood yellow fully once all the physics and physiology were accounted for, so this argument is aimed against physicalism, not for it. Behaviorism and structuralism are historical methods for studying the mind, not answers to the mind-body problem.
hint: Ask whether experiencing the color adds anything beyond the complete physical facts — and which camp says 'yes.'

q: Which philosopher argued that the mind is based on an immortal soul that is more 'real' than the physical world?
explain: Plato (around 350 B.C.) argued the mind rests on an immortal soul more real than the physical world. Descartes came later and made the mind/matter distinction precise, locating their interaction at the pineal gland, but is not the one tying mind to an immortal soul in this way. Jackson devised the modern knowledge argument. Watson founded behaviorism and rejected talk of internal mental states entirely.
hint: This is the earliest thinker mentioned, from ancient times, who tied the mind to a soul.

q: A patient with damage to a visual processing area sincerely reports being unable to see anything, yet when asked to guess whether a light appeared in the left or right visual field, answers correctly far above chance. This pattern is best described as:
explain: This is blindsight from cortical blindness: damage before conscious perception leaves patients reporting no sight yet responding to visual stimuli above chance, showing processing outside awareness. A speed-accuracy tradeoff involves trading response speed for accuracy on tasks the person can perform consciously. Latent learning and classical conditioning are behaviorist phenomena unrelated to unconscious vision.
hint: The person insists they can't see, but their guesses reveal the eyes are working.

q: An investigator studying how people solve a difficult puzzle asks participants to speak aloud everything they are thinking while they work through it. This modern research technique, which descends from introspection, is called the:
explain: Having participants report their thought processes during a complex task is the think-aloud protocol, a legacy of introspection that survives in modern research. A Skinner box is an operant conditioning chamber for animals. A go/no-go task is Donders' discrimination condition. Signal detection is a framework for decisions under uncertainty, not a self-report method.
hint: The participant narrates their reasoning out loud as it happens.

q: Who is credited with founding the first scientific psychology laboratory and establishing experimental psychology?
explain: Wilhelm Wundt founded the first scientific psychology lab and is associated with the birth of experimental psychology. Titchener was Wundt's student who brought structuralism to America, not the lab's founder. Watson founded behaviorism, a later and opposing approach. Donders studied reaction time as an early cognitivist, not a structuralist lab founder.
hint: Think about who ran the very first lab, not the student who exported the ideas.

q: Artificial neural networks are inspired by:
options: Only vacuum tubes with no learning
Networks of simple units with weighted connections that can learn from data
explain: ANNs: units, weights, learning rules — abstractly inspired by neural computation.
hint: ANNs: units, weights, learning rules — abstractly inspired by neural computation.

q: Which lobe is most associated with vision?
options: Parietal only for speech production always
explain: Primary visual cortex is occipital.
hint: Primary visual cortex is occipital.

q: The frontal lobe is especially important for:
options: Motor control and executive/planning functions among other roles
explain: Motor cortex + prefrontal executive functions live frontally.
hint: Motor cortex + prefrontal executive functions live frontally.

q: fMRI roughly measures:
options: Blood-oxygen-level–dependent signals related to neural activity indirectly
Only single spikes always with perfect isolation
explain: Hemodynamic proxy.
hint: Hemodynamic proxy.

q: Neurons communicate primarily by:
options: Electrical activity within cells and chemical transmission at synapses
Only radio waves outside the skull always
explain: Action potentials + synaptic neurotransmitter signaling are core.
hint: Action potentials + synaptic neurotransmitter signaling are core.

q: A feature detector neuron is tuned to:
explain: Classic Hubel/Wiesel-style idea: selective responses to features; combinations build complexity.
hint: Classic Hubel/Wiesel-style idea: selective responses to features; combinations build complexity.

q: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the:
explain: CNS = brain + spinal cord; PNS is outside that.
hint: CNS = brain + spinal cord; PNS is outside that.

q: The autonomic nervous system primarily regulates:
options: Only finger tapping for a piano recital choice
explain: Autonomic = involuntary; further divides into sympathetic and parasympathetic.
hint: Autonomic = involuntary; further divides into sympathetic and parasympathetic.

q: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are especially successful at:
options: Visual classification tasks using hierarchical local filters
explain: CNNs stack local receptive-field-like operations — loosely inspired by visual hierarchy.
hint: CNNs stack local receptive-field-like operations — loosely inspired by visual hierarchy.

q: Sensation vs perception: sensation is closer to ____; perception is closer to ____.
options: Raw sensory transduction/registration; organized interpretation of that input
explain: Sensation: physical energy → neural signals. Perception: constructing useful representations/experience.
hint: Sensation: physical energy → neural signals. Perception: constructing useful representations/experie

q: Two people can receive similar sensory input but perceive differently because:
options: Perception is purely the retina with no brain
Perception involves constructive interpretation influenced by knowledge/context
explain: Perception is not a passive photograph; top-down factors shape interpretation.
hint: Perception is not a passive photograph; top-down factors shape interpretation.

q: Transduction means:
options: Converting physical stimulus energy into neural signals
explain: Each sense has receptors that transduce a preferred energy form.
hint: Each sense has receptors that transduce a preferred energy form.

q: Bottom-up processing emphasizes:
options: Building perception from sensory input features
explain: Data-driven analysis of the stimulus.
hint: Data-driven analysis of the stimulus.

q: Gestalt psychologists emphasized that:
options: The whole can be different from the sum of parts; organization principles matter
Perception is only pixel lists with no structure
explain: Grouping laws (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, etc.) organize scenes.
hint: Grouping laws (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, etc.) organize scenes.

q: Unconscious inference (Helmholtz) suggests perception involves:
options: Making best guesses about the world from incomplete sensory data
explain: The brain infers likely distal causes of proximal stimulation.
hint: The brain infers likely distal causes of proximal stimulation.

q: Depth cues can be monocular or binocular; binocular disparity relies on:
options: Slightly different images in the two eyes
explain: Stereopsis uses disparity between left and right retinal images.
hint: Stereopsis uses disparity between left and right retinal images.

q: Endogenous attention is:
options: Only a reflex to loud noise
explain: You choose to attend (cues, goals, search).
hint: You choose to attend (cues, goals, search).

q: Divided attention refers to:
options: Attempting to perform/monitor multiple tasks or inputs at once
explain: Multitasking; often incurs dual-task costs.
hint: Multitasking; often incurs dual-task costs.

q: Early-selection theories propose the attentional filter acts:
options: Only after full semantic analysis of all inputs
Before full meaning is analyzed (based on physical features)
explain: Broadbent-style early filter: selection on sensory channels before deep processing.
hint: Broadbent-style early filter: selection on sensory channels before deep processing.

q: Treisman’s attenuation theory suggests unattended messages are:
options: Turned down (attenuated) rather than fully eliminated; important words can still break through
explain: Attenuation: reduced analysis, not absolute early block — cocktail party name effects fit.
hint: Attenuation: reduced analysis, not absolute early block — cocktail party name effects fit.

q: Dichotic listening experiments typically present:
options: The same message to both ears always
Different messages to each ear while shadowing one
explain: Classic tool for selective attention: shadow attended ear; probe what leaks from unattended.
hint: Classic tool for selective attention: shadow attended ear; probe what leaks from unattended.

q: Inattentional blindness refers to:
options: Failing to notice an unexpected stimulus when attention is engaged elsewhere
explain: Classic demos (e.g., unexpected events during a demanding task) show perception depends on attention.
hint: Classic demos (e.g., unexpected events during a demanding task) show perception depends on attention

q: Change blindness shows that:
options: We always detect every visual change instantly
Large changes can go unnoticed without attention/comparison
explain: Without attention and comparison processes, even big scene changes can be missed.
hint: Without attention and comparison processes, even big scene changes can be missed.

q: Perceptual load theory (Lavie) claims that processing of distractors depends on:
options: How much capacity the main task consumes
Only the day of the week
explain: High load can exhaust capacity so distractors get less processing; low load leaves spare capacity.
hint: High load can exhaust capacity so distractors get less processing; low load leaves spare capacity.

q: Feature integration theory (Treisman) proposes that attention is needed to:
options: Only detect a single pop-out feature always without binding
explain: Features may be registered preattentively; binding into objects needs focused attention — illusory conjunctions when attention is diverted.
hint: Features may be registered preattentively; binding into objects needs focused attention — illusory c
